title: React
description: Build UIs with components, JSX, props, state, hooks, and effects.

lessons.title: React Introduction
lessons.subtitle: Components, the virtual DOM, and declarative UI
lessons.theory:
React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces from composable components. You describe what the UI should look like for a given state; React updates the DOM efficiently.

Components are functions or classes that return JSX — syntax that looks like HTML inside JavaScript.

The virtual DOM diffing algorithm minimizes expensive direct DOM writes.

React fits single-page apps, design systems, and islands of interactivity inside larger sites.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: React is a declarative UI library where reusable components render output from props and state.

keyPoints: Works with Vite, Next.js, and more

quiz.question: What does React primarily help you build?
quiz.explanation: React focuses on view layers and component-based UIs.

quiz.question: What concept does React use to decide when the UI should change?
quiz.explanation: React re-renders when state or props change.

quiz.question: What kind of code do React components usually return?
quiz.explanation: Components typically return JSX to describe UI structure.

lessons.title: JSX
lessons.subtitle: Embedding markup and expressions in components
lessons.theory:
JSX lets you write HTML-like tags in JavaScript. It must compile (Vite, Babel) before browsers run it. Embed expressions with curly braces:

{count}

. Attributes use camelCase: className, htmlFor. One parent element per return is required unless you use a Fragment: <>.... Self-closing tags need a slash: .

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: JSX is a syntax extension that describes UI structure as XML-like tags within JavaScript source files.

quiz.question: Which attribute replaces class in JSX?
quiz.explanation: className maps to the DOM class attribute because class is reserved in JavaScript.

quiz.question: Why must JSX usually return one parent element?
options: Because JSX compiles into a single expression
quiz.explanation: A component return must resolve to one root element or a Fragment.

quiz.question: What is a Fragment used for in React?
options: To avoid extra wrapper DOM nodes
quiz.explanation: Fragments group children without adding unnecessary DOM elements.

lessons.title: Props
lessons.subtitle: Passing data from parent to child
lessons.theory:
Props are read-only inputs to a component, like function arguments. The parent passes them as attributes: <Card title="HTML" />.

Destructure in the parameter list: function Card({ title }) { ... }.

Props flow one way: down the tree. Children should not mutate props.

Default values can use default parameters or defaultProps in older patterns.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Props are the external configuration a parent component supplies to a child component.

keyPoints: Props are immutable for the child
Spread rest props onto DOM elements carefully
Type or document props in larger apps

codeExample.label: Props

quiz.question: Can a child component change its props directly?
quiz.explanation: Props are owned by the parent; the child should treat them as immutable.

quiz.question: What is the usual direction of props flow?
quiz.explanation: Props are passed down the component tree.

quiz.question: What prop pattern is often used to pass nested UI?
quiz.explanation: The children prop carries nested JSX into a component.

lessons.title: State with useState
lessons.subtitle: Local data that changes over time
lessons.theory:
State is data a component remembers between renders. useState returns a value and a setter: const [count, setCount] = useState(0).

Calling the setter schedules a re-render with the new value. Do not mutate state objects directly — create new copies.

State updates may batch for performance. Functional updates help when the next value depends on the previous: setCount(c => c + 1).

Lift state up when siblings need to share data.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: State is mutable component memory that triggers re-renders when updated through the provided setter function.

keyPoints: Lift shared state to common parent

codeExample.label: Counter

quiz.question: What hook adds local state to a function component?
quiz.explanation: useState is the primary hook for local component state.

quiz.question: Why should state objects be replaced immutably?
options: Because mutation is faster in React
Because React detects updates by new references
quiz.explanation: Creating a new object or array helps React see that state changed.

quiz.question: What form of setState is useful when the next value depends on the previous one?
quiz.explanation: Functional updates avoid stale state when updates are queued.

lessons.title: useEffect
lessons.subtitle: Side effects after render
lessons.theory:
useEffect runs code after paint — fetching data, subscribing to events, or syncing with non-React APIs.

Pass a dependency array to control when it re-runs: useEffect(fn, [id]) runs when id changes. An empty array runs once on mount.

Return a cleanup function to unsubscribe or cancel timers on unmount or before re-running.

Keep effects focused; heavy logic may belong in event handlers or data libraries.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: An effect is work that connects a component to the outside world, scheduled after React commits DOM updates.

keyPoints: Do not fetch in render body — use effects or loaders

codeExample.label: Document title effect

quiz.question: When does useEffect run relative to paint?
quiz.explanation: Effects run after the browser paints the updated UI (in the default layout effect timing for useEffect).

quiz.question: What should you return from an effect that sets up a subscription?
quiz.explanation: Cleanup prevents leaks and removes subscriptions or timers.

quiz.question: What does an empty dependency array usually mean?
options: Run the effect once on mount
quiz.explanation: An empty array means the effect runs after the first render only.
